per_user_turn.result_preview:
The interceptor now accepts a `TimingInterceptorOptions` object with an optional `headerName` field (defaults to `'X-Response-Time'`). The registration in `core.module.ts` uses `useFactory` to pass options at construction time. To use a different header name, just change the factory:

```ts
useFactory: () => new TimingInterceptor({ headerName: 'X-Request-Duration' }),
```

per_user_turn.result_preview:
`threshold` (in ms) defaults to `0`, so existing behaviour is preserved. The header is only set when `elapsed >= threshold`. To only add it for slow requests, pass e.g.:

```ts
useFactory: () => new TimingInterceptor({ headerName: 'X-Response-Time', threshold: 100 }),
```

per_user_turn.result_preview:
The interceptor now accepts a `TimingInterceptorOptions` object in its constructor:

- **`headerName`** — the response header to set (defaults to `'X-Response-Time'`)

Usage examples:
```ts
// Default header name
new TimingInterceptor()

// Custom header name
new TimingInterceptor({ headerName: 'X-Request-Duration' })
```

In `core.module.ts`, `useFactory` is used instead of `useClass` so the options object can be passed at registration time.

Created `sample/01-cats-app/src/common/interceptors/timing.interceptor.ts`.

**How it works:**
- Records `Date.now()` before the handler runs
- Uses `tap` (which runs on both success and error completion) to set the `X-Response-Time` header with the elapsed milliseconds
- `setHeader` is called on the HTTP response after the handler resolves

**To use it**, apply it globally in `main.ts` or per-controller/route:

```typescript
// Global (main.ts)
app.useGlobalInterceptors(new TimingInterceptor())

per_user_turn.result_preview:
Done. Here's what was added:

**New file: `packages/common/interceptors/response-time.interceptor.ts`**

The `ResponseTimeInterceptor` implements `NestInterceptor` and:
1. Records `Date.now()` before calling `next.handle()`
2. Uses RxJS `tap` to set the `X-Response-Time: <N>ms` header after the handler resolves
3. Supports both Express (`setHeader`) and Fastify (`header`) response APIs

**Usage in a NestJS app:**

```ts
// Global (main.ts)
app.useGlobalInterceptors(new ResponseTimeInterceptor())

**`packages/common/interceptors/request-timing.interceptor.ts`** — The `RequestTimingInterceptor` class:
- Records `Date.now()` before calling `next.handle()`
- Uses `tap()` to run after the response stream completes
- Guards with `context.getType() === 'http'` so it's a no-op in microservice/WebSocket contexts
- Sets `X-Response-Time: <n>ms` on the response via `setHeader`

per_user_turn.result_preview:
The header is now only set when `elapsed > threshold`. When `threshold` is omitted the behaviour is unchanged (header always added). Example:

```typescript
// Only add the header for requests slower than 200ms
new RequestTimingInterceptor({ threshold: 200 })
```

Created `sample/01-cats-app/src/common/interceptors/timing.interceptor.ts`.

**How it works:**

1. Records `Date.now()` at the start of `intercept`
2. Gets the HTTP response object via `context.switchToHttp().getResponse()`
3. Uses `tap` (side-effect operator) to set the `X-Response-Time` header after the handler completes, with the elapsed time in milliseconds

```ts

app.useGlobalInterceptors(new TimingI

per_user_turn.result_preview:
`TimingInterceptor` now accepts an optional `TimingInterceptorOptions` object with a `headerName` property (defaults to `'X-Response-Time'`). To use a custom header name, pass it when instantiating:

```typescript
new TimingInterceptor({ headerName: 'X-Request-Duration' })
```

tools.name: Edit
Edit

per_user_turn.result_preview:
`threshold` (in ms) defaults to `0`, meaning the header is always added. Set it to suppress the header for fast responses — e.g. only report slow requests:

```typescript
new TimingInterceptor({ threshold: 200 }) // header only added when > 200ms
```

app.useGlobalInterceptors(new TimingInterceptor());

per_user_turn.result_preview:
Created `sample/01-cats-app/src/common/interceptors/timing.interceptor.ts`.

**How it works:**
- Records `Date.now()` before the handler runs
- Uses `tap` (side-effect only, doesn't alter the stream) to set the `X-Response-Time` header after the handler completes
- The header value is in milliseconds, e.g. `X-Response-Time: 42ms`

**Usage** — apply globally in `main.ts`, at the module level, or on specific controllers/routes:

```ts
// Global (main.ts)
app.useGlobalInterceptors(new TimingInterce
